Hopefully this is the correct way to report a flock? There were only just two at first (having some suet at my patio feeder) but then ten minutes later an entire flock of at least 150 European Starlings came right over to settle / fly in circles / make some calls in the set of tall trees lining my parking lot. I used my binoculars to check what kind of blackbirds they were at first, and then the recordings I took verified it for me :) so cool to watch from my patio!

Early in the morning I woke up to a bird call I had never heard before. It was complex, unique, and filled with various sounds. I went back to sleep. Then in the afternoon it started again. I kept thinking maybe it was someone's pet bird? I couldn't help but go outside and see what bird was creating these sounds. I found it perched on a wall. It kept flying between three locations. I looked it up and discovered it  appears to be a Starling. I was born and raised here in LA and never remember having heard this call. Also I saw a crow chasing it which was interesting. Apologies for the poor quality as they are screenshots from a video. I didn't have a professional camera.

Observation Details: Saw and heard two individuals perched in small crabapple trees, eating berries, almost due east of parking spot, as I got out of my car. Later observed a group of 6 individuals perched on wire or wooden pole of large power poles along paved path, a bit south of 30th Ave S. ... (One individual was carrying a crabapple in its beak, and at one point other individuals flew over at it, trying to steal the fruit. It managed to keep it for a moment, but then dropped it, despite its attempts to catch it as it fell from the top bar of the power pole!) ... Later, saw the same individuals -- three perched at SE edge of roof and the other three at NE edge of roof of Scheels Arena, west of my parking spot. (Posted audio recording of the 6 individuals making various calls, just before they all flew off.)

Media notes: 4 EUST were recorded as they sang from a tree near a barn. Mimics: AMRO 12.75, 4:01, GRCA: 37.5,. COLO: 41, EAME: 3:07.5, EABL: 3:11.5 and  KILL-like at 3 seconds Unique note at 1:20.25 on the recording.
Observation Details: EUST were noted at various locations on this checklist. Four were noted on the top of a tree next to an old barn and were quite vocal (recorded). Others were perched on the peak of the barn roof and on the nearby trees. This barn was the site for nesting for EUST last year. Of interest, to my ear, there were a number of species mimicked by the EUST throughout the recording.  Mimics: AMRO 12.75, 4:01, GRCA: 37.5,. COLO: 41, EAME: 3:07.5, EABL: 3:11.5 and  KILL-like at 3 seconds Unique note at 1:20.25 on the recording which I am not familiar with. Amazing vocalists.

Area is in a subdivision with houses, near the edge of a small hill. But just a couple weeks later, a different EUST was doing a perfect Common Nighthawk imitation (and spooked before being recorded unfortunately) confirming that they are indeed master mimics

I try not to speculate, only send in the facts but it's interesting.

Media notes: EUST recorded after they were disturbed.
Observation Details: EUST were observed as they perched high in a tree near the feeder area. This has been the usual roost for them to gather at. They were very vocal tonight. Recording one, was of the 14 EUST as they vocalized together. This group had several  soft "mimic-notes" of other species. Mimic of KILL (7-9), EAME (31,39), AMRO (35.8), EABL(43.5) seconds were noted to my ears in the field (also COLO once, not on the recording). Examples of whistles (13), and "flapping series" (14.5) seconds were noted. I moved to get closer (40 meters) to record them again and 6 birds left. The remaining 8 birds seemed startled and were silent for almost minute. They then started to vocalize again with long pauses noted between the intermittent note, recording two. Sunset was about 5:59 PM today.

Media notes: I add this audio because it is of interest - see checklist note for the species for more info.
Observation Details: I have uploaded audio because I have found this call of interest: the same call was heard on both my prior visits (30 April after 2pm and yesterday, 18 May after 6:40am) from exactly the same spot.  On the prior visits a single bird was giving the call from the very dense shrubbery and trees below tall eucalypts beside the grandstand of the abandoned stadium above the wetland. The location is very localised - surrounded by considerable grassy areas in all directions. However it was unusual to have just one (and today two) birds making this call - without any other call that would readily be identified as Common Starling - while anything up to about 15 other Starlings were perched very high in the eucalypts making no calls. Additionally, today I heard the call while I was still approaching the grandstand, from easily 50 metres away.  The ID is made today on the basis of this louder call - there is a high pitched metallic squeaky/scraping sound - this is a property I have heard in the background of an extensive song given by a Common Starling in Tasmania (with audio uploaded against checklist https://ebird.org/checklist/S55147063 ).  The calls here at Landing Lights have been extremely localised and although the birds appeared to come nearer when I played back recordings of this call, they simply would not show themselves - remaining secreted in the shrubs. I wonder whether this might be part of a courtship ritual with two birds setting up nest.

Media notes: Several EUST coming in to roost for the evening. They climbed up into the upper rafters at the peak of the roof. It appeared that they were climbing in between the rafters and the sheet metal. They came in through the south barn door in a couple groups and stayed at the south end of the barn. Unlike the northern group (see other audio), they were quite noisy for awhile as they (presumably) "fought" for space in their roosting area.

Locality: Bear Run Village Apartments - Pittsburgh PA
Observation Details: Groups of 1, 5 and 13. The one alone was mimicking a red shouldered hawk and maybe also a chickadee. One in the group of 5 was mimicking a red tailed hawk and a killdeer. The killdeer call was very good…sounded authentic to me. The red tailed sounded weak and airy…not very convincing. I could not figure out whether it was also mimicking a cowbird or whether there was actually a cowbird in the tree. Fascinating to listen to their imitations. The house finch, robins and wrens in the audio were not starling imitations.

Media notes: When I heard this sound - a convincing "chip vireo chip" - I believed it to be an early White-eyed Vireo in appropriate habitat, as the sound was coming from the Northern Virginia Conservation Trust property where this species can be located sporadically in breeding season.  Due to rapidly diminishing daylight, I did not attempt to locate the individual, but instead decided to proceed with my usual birding route.  After further consideration and research, I've concluded that it is likely that the vocalizing individual was a European Starling. There were a lot of "whistle call notes" before and after the recording. Sunset was about 17:54 hours tonight at this location.

Media notes: Heard intricate, melodic songs from a group of trees at a Trailhead at the edge of a prairie. Then saw several individuals (at least five, but there were likely more). ... (This audio recording captured some individuals mimicking calls of different species -- including the calls of a Gull species, songs of a Western Meadowlark, and the scream call of a Red-Tailed Hawk [which I am certain I heard mimicked in my previous outing in the same area].)
Observation Details: Heard intricate, melodic songs as I approached the South Trailhead as I returned to my car. Then saw several individuals (I saw at least five, but there were likely more), which explained the songs. ... I was also able to confirm -- and record -- some individuals mimicking W Meadowlark songs and RT Hawk calls (which explained why I heard the call so close, but couldn't see a hawk!). ... (Took a photo of one or two individuals at top of tree, to post eventually. Also made audio recording of a group singing at same site. Made another audio recording that included mimicry of W Meadowlark song & RT Hawk calls!)

Media notes: I am confident that this call -- a clear, loud "screaming" call of a Red-Tailed Hawk, heard from the trees nearby, was actually a mimicked call made by one of a couple of European Starlings seen perched in those trees. ... (I could not see any hawk, perched or flying). ... (Later, after returning to South Trailhead at the end of my second list/outing, I heard -- and recorded -- E Starlings mimicking W Meadowlark songs and RT Hawk calls!)
Observation Details: Saw the shadow moving past me before I saw one individual flying directly overhead to the top part of a tall tree, just south of South Trailhead. When I was about to take a picture, it flew, but then a second individual joined it, they circled, and then flew away to the south. ... I heard a clear, loud "screaming" call of a Red-Tailed Hawk in the trees here, but couldn't see any hawk (perched or flying). (Later, after returning to South Trailhead at the end of my second list/outing, I heard -- and recorded -- E Starlings mimicking W Meadowlark songs and RT Hawk calls!) ... (Posted an audio recording of the last call I heard, which I am quite sure is a mimicked call made by a E Starling.)

In cottonwoods 10-20 m.  Imitations include KILL, NOFL, RWBL, WEME, and some sort of catbird thing.

Two birds perched very close together (moving about a bit, but sometimes touching) in trees by the horse shed, both singing, with a third perched nearby. Song incorporated fairly clear imitations of howling coyotes, Red-tailed Hawk, Bald Eagle, Canada Goose (distant-sounding honks), American Wigeon, Mallard, Black-bellied Plover (“Pyowee” call), Killdeer (both “kill-dee” and trilling calls), Solitary Sandpiper (remarkably loud and clear calls!), Northern flicker (laughing-type call), Eastern Phoebe (song), American Robin (clucking call notes, and some very faint song), Gray catbird (mewing call), Boreal Chickadee (I guess it could be a poorly imitated black-capped, but sounds remarkably good for Boreal), and Yellow Warbler, and blackbird call notes. Possibly also call notes from lesser yellowlegs, pine siskin, and American crow (though I wasn’t sure on these three).

Observation Details: The second recording is a long sequence of very cool mimics.  I have noted some of the more obvious mimics, other calls are there but are not as obvious.  The first call is A California Quail, then most likely a SWTH call following into a jumble of American Robin, Killdeer, House sparrow and Pacific Wren calls. At 16.5 seconds it changes to Song Sparrow “chimp” calls and Brown headed cowbird, a little Red Winged Blackbird mixed in because why not. 39.5 seconds is the classic Red-tailed hawk. Following the RTHA calls is an amazing HY Glaucous winged gull call.  50s starts with maybe an Anna's hummingbird song. 54.5 is house sparrow chatter, followed by maybe a chicken or rooster? Then more Song sparrow and Brown headed cowbird. 1.27 is a nice Red winged Black bird. 1.48 is a great California Quail. 2.01 has 8 Mallard quacks. 221.0 is a nice Cedar Waxwing. 3.35.5 is are awesome Bushtit alarm Calls, First time I have heard this one from EUST .   3.44 Are three repetitions of Spotted sandpiper. Not as obvious as some but its there. 4.14 there are two vocalizations that sound almost like a human voice, speculation of course. 5.41 looks to be a Brown creeper or GCKI. 6.35 is Purple finch flight call.

Un groupe de 70 individus perchés dans des grands arbres. Descendent périodiquement au sol ou sur un tas de foin pour s’alimenter. Très vocaux, imitent entre autres des cris de moineau domestique, roselins pourpré et familier, et pic flamboyant. J’ai joint à la liste un enregistrement où on entend des imitations de moineau domestique. Les espèces mentionnées n’ont pas été vues pendant les 20 minutes d’observation aux jumelles et télescope à 20 mètres de distance. De plus, les cris de ces espèces semblaient toujours provenir de là où le groupe d’étourneaux se trouvait.

Observation Details: Estimate by tens, counted in flight, early in the survey in reach 4. I had several opportunities to count the flock this way, corroborating my estimate (consistently getting around 300). There may have been more, as the whole flock was not necessarily in the air/visible at the same time interval.  I counted a sample of 50 birds perched along the creek, of which 44 were grayish juveniles and the other 6 were adult-aspect birds.  I saw the large flock only in the eastern part of the transect early; I saw groups of 10-30 in flight along the creek corridor numerous times later in the morning.
